test: FX interface Line B — 7-currency panel (real data)
key_findings: Direction holds: anchored currencies less volatile on average (2.2x), but WEAKER and messier than the 2-currency pass (12x) suggested. Adding data corrected an over-clean result.
OVERLAP: DKK (anchored, 7.47%) > CAD (floating, 6.09%). The anchored/floating line is not clean.

METHODOLOGICAL CATCH: DKK is anchored to the EUR, not the USD — so vs USD it inherits EUR/USD volatility and looks like a floater. 'Anchored' is relative to the anchor target. Only HKD (hard board vs USD) is truly USD-anchored, hence its extreme 0.90%.

CS IMPLICATION (refined): a CS currency anchors INFLATION and floats nominally — so vs USD it behaves like a credibly-inflation-anchored FLOATER, not a hard peg. The realistic CS FX-stability effect is MODEST (~2x range), not dramatic (12x). Only a hard nominal USD peg gets HKD-like stability, which CS is NOT.
honest_caveats: Still short window (2021-2026) including the 2022 dollar surge — inflates floating vols.
n=2 anchored (and one, HKD, is USD-pegged; the other, DKK, is EUR-pegged) — the 'anchored' group is not homogeneous. Needs USD-relevant anchors and more of them.
Line A (PPP/UIP decomposition) still not run — needs partner CPI + policy rates.
Vol vs USD conflates 'anchor to USD' with 'anchor to anything' — a cleaner design would measure vol vs the currency each is actually anchored to, plus vs a common numeraire.
verdict: Real, honest first panel result. The anchor effect is REAL but MODEST (~2x) once you go beyond the HKD hard-peg extreme. This is a more defensible — and more useful — number than the 12x two-currency teaser.
